title: practical english usage - Part 288
content:
We   use ago with a past tense and a time expression to ‘ count back’  from the countingback 4 ago     and   before       with    time    expressions: He was ill for three years before he died. (= His illness lasted three years.) not                                                     or (     He died for three years,  ... for three years ago.) He died three years ago. (= three years before now) a past tense) says how  long it lasted. Compare: Ago says how  long before the present something happened; for (with 3 the   difference        between       ago     and   for ’ Where's Mike?' ’ He was working outside ten minutes ago.' not She phoned a few minutes ago. (     She has phoned. ..) a past tense, not a present perfect (►  47.4).

An expression with ago refers to a finished time, and is normally used with 2 tenses not I met her six weeks ago. (     ..                                   a long time ago Ago follows an expression of time.

1  word    order:    six    weeks       ago 362 ago    ___________________ ______________ ► again      and   back      ɜθo not went home, (     . . .after all we went home.) After the theatre we had supper and went to a nightclub; then we finally After all does not mean ‘ finally’ , ‘ at last’ , ‘ in the end’ .

2 not   used    for   'finally' Let's finish the cake. Somebody's got to eat it, after all.

Of  course you're tired. After all, you were up all night.

or end of a clause.

or reason which  may have been forgotten. Position: at the beginning Another meaning is ‘ we  mustn’ t forget that...’ , introducing an argument I expected to fail the exam, but I passed after all.

I'm sorry. I know I said I would help you, but I can 't after all.

was expected’ . Position: usually at the end of a clause.

After all can mean ‘ in spite of what was said before’  or ‘ contrary to what 1  two    meanings 361 after      all  __________________________ 365 vocabulary' •         allow, permit and let Smoking is not allowed/permitted in the kitchen.             -> People are not allowed/permitted to smoke in the kitchen.

subjects are both possible.

Passive structures are common; personal subjects and gerund (-ing form) We do not allow/permit smoking in the kitchen.

When  there is no personal object, an -ing form is used.

We do not ɑllow/permit people to smoke in the kitchen.

can be followed by object + infinitive.

These words have similar meanings and uses. Permit is more formal. Both words 1  allow      and   permit 365 allow,      permit        and     let people consider it incorrect.

The  standard spelling is all right. Alright is common in informal usage, but some 364 all   right      and   alright not He's got two very similar-looking daughters, (     ... alike daughters.) His two daughters are very much  alike.

Alike is not often used before a noun (►    183). Compare: not He's like his brother, (     He's alike-his brother.) The two boys are alike in looks, but not in personality.

The  adjective alike means 'like each other’ . Compare: 363 alike     _________________________ ►    ɜʒʒ agree      and   accept For other uses of before, ►    250, 397.

ten years before                           three years ago years before.

I met her three years ago. I had been at school with her husband ten and ago        before not ten years before, (     ... ten years agp.) When we got talking I found out that I had been at school with her husband Word  Problems from A  to Z      Section 31 vocabulary’  •  366   almost and nearly; practically Almost nobody / hardly anybody was there.

She's almost never / hardly ever at home, (     . . . nearly never...) anything, etc.

nobody, nothing. Instead, we  use almost, or we  use hardly with ever, anybody, We   do not usually use nearly before negative pronouns or adverbs like never, 3 never,      nobody,        nothing,         etc Jake is almost like a father to me.

I almost wish I'd stayed at home, (     J-nearly-wish-. . .) not (     . . . She nearly-soundsfloreign?) My aunt's got a strange accent. She almost sounds foreign.
